content.problemStatement:
This is the easy version of the problem. The only difference between this and the hard version are the constraints on NNN: here, N‚Č§90N \le 90N‚Č§90 and the sum of N4N^4N4 won't exceed 90490^4904.

For a permutation‚Ä†^\dagger‚Ä† PPP of the integers {1,2,‚Ä¶,N}\{1, 2, \ldots, N\}{1,2,‚Ä¶,N}, we define f(P)f(P)f(P) to be the length of the longest increasing subsequence of consecutive elements in PPP.

Formally, f(P)f(P)f(P) is defined to be the largest integer KKK such that there exists a sequence i1,i2,‚Ä¶,iKi_1, i_2, \ldots, i_Ki1‚Äč,i2‚Äč,‚Ä¶,iK‚Äč satisfying:


1‚Č§i1<i2<‚Ä¶<iK‚Č§N1 \le i_1 \lt i_2 \lt\ldots\lt i_K \le N1‚Č§i1‚Äč<i2‚Äč<‚Ä¶<iK‚Äč‚Č§N, and
Pij=Pij‚ąí1+1P_{i_j} = P_{i_{j-1}} + 1Pij‚Äč‚Äč=Pij‚ąí1‚Äč‚Äč+1 for each 1<j‚Č§K1 \lt j \le K1<j‚Č§K
For example, if P=[3,2,4,1,5]P = [3, 2, 4, 1, 5]P=[3,2,4,1,5], then f(P)=3f(P) = 3f(P)=3 because of the subsequence [3,4,5][3, 4, 5][3,4,5].

‚Ä†^\dagger‚Ä†A permutation of the integers {1,2,‚Ä¶,N}\{1, 2, \ldots, N\}{1,2,‚Ä¶,N} is an array of length NNN that contains every integer from 111 to NNN exactly once each.

You're given a permutation PPP of {1,2,‚Ä¶,N}\{1, 2, \ldots, N\}{1,2,‚Ä¶,N}.

You start with 000 coins. You can perform the following operation however many times you like (possibly, zero times):


Pay one coin and swap two adjacent elements of PPP.

That is, pick an index iii such that 1‚Č§i<N1 \le i \lt N1‚Č§i<N and perform swap(Pi,Pi+1)\text{swap}(P_i, P_{i+1})swap(Pi‚Äč,Pi+1‚Äč).
After performing however many swaps you like, you will receive f(P)f(P)f(P) coins in the end (where PPP here is the final state of the permutation, after swaps).

Find the **maximum** possible number of coins you can have in the end.

Note that you may have negative coins in intermediate steps (while performing swaps), which is fine - we only care about maximizing the final number of coins we have.


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of two lines of input.

The first line of each test case contains a single integer NNN ‚ÄĒ the length of the permutation PPP.
The second line contains NNN space-separated integers P1,‚Ä¶,PNP_1, \ldots, P_NP1‚Äč,‚Ä¶,PN‚Äč




Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line the maximum number of coins you can have in the end if you perform swaps optimally.
constraints: The sum of N4N^4N4 over all test cases won't exceed 90490^4904.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 111: It's optimal to not perform any swaps. f(P)=2f(P) = 2f(P)=2, so the answer is 2‚ąí0=22 - 0 = 22‚ąí0=2.
Test case 222: For P=[2,1]P = [2, 1]P=[2,1], we have f(P)=1f(P) = 1f(P)=1.
We can perform one swap and obtain P=[1,2]P = [1, 2]P=[1,2] which has f(P)=2f(P) = 2f(P)=2; but we'll still only receive 2‚ąí1=12-1 = 12‚ąí1=1 coin.
So, the answer is 111.
Test case 333: It's optimal to swap the first two elements to obtain P=[1,2,3]P = [1, 2, 3]P=[1,2,3].
This gives us f(P)‚ąí1=3‚ąí1=2f(P) - 1 = 3 - 1 = 2f(P)‚ąí1=3‚ąí1=2 coins, which is optimal.
